story.commons_artist: United States. Bureau of Land Management
story.description: A clip related to natural science, biology, research, fossils, or discovery: stunning interior of historic museum
story.hook: Discoveries show the first clue before the payoff.
story.lead: Meet the creature inside this fossil. That bony frill isn’t armor—it’s a heat radiator. Tiny hollow tubes inside pumped blood to cool a brain that ran hotter than a mammal’s. That’s why this dino kept its cool while chasing prey. Ever seen a skull built like a car’s radiator? Watch this one twice.
story.script: Meet the creature inside this fossil. That bony frill isn’t armor—it’s a heat radiator. Tiny hollow tubes inside pumped blood to cool a brain that ran hotter than a mammal’s. That’s why this dino kept its cool while chasing prey. Ever seen a skull built like a car’s radiator? Watch this one twice.

story.description: A clip of rivers, streams, currents, waterfalls, or freshwater systems: a view of the flooded fields from the air
story.lead: Flood rivers build gardens fast. Watch the muddy swirls spread. That's because silt drops first, right where plants need it. New sprouts pop up in days. Ever see a field turn into a pond overnight? Tiny comment question.

story.description: A clip of marine life in the ocean: vibrant underwater life in aquarium exhibit
story.hook: Sharks show the eyes before the payoff.
story.lead: Sharks smell blood first. That's why they don't need to see you. Watch their heads twitch side to side—that's tracking scent trails underwater. Their nostrils sit on the snout tip like two tiny funnels. Blood hits one nostril a second before the other and *bam*—the shark knows exactly where to turn. Did you ever wonder how they hunt in murky water? Smell does the job.
story.script: Sharks smell blood first. That's why they don't need to see you. Watch their heads twitch side to side—that's tracking scent trails underwater. Their nostrils sit on the snout tip like two tiny funnels. Blood hits one nostril a second before the other and *bam*—the shark knows exactly where to turn. Did you ever wonder how they hunt in murky water? Smell does the job.
